curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: cosine rule for non-right-angled triangles (SAS and SSS)
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Use the Cosine Rule to calculate missing sides (SAS) and missing angles (SSS) in any triangle, connecting the formula to Pythagoras’ Theorem and justifying solutions in real design contexts.

verse_ality.driving_question: What’s always true about the Cosine Rule, and how does it extend Pythagoras’ Theorem to any triangle?

knowing_world.focus: Learn how the Cosine Rule generalises Pythagoras’ Theorem for any triangle, and practise using both forms to calculate missing sides and angles.
key_concepts: Cosine Rule for a missing side: a² = b² + c² − 2bc cos(A)
Cosine Rule for a missing angle: cos(A) = (b² + c² − a²) / (2bc)
Link to Pythagoras’ Theorem (right angle special case)
tools: Video: Deriving and using the Cosine Rule (SAS) (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAGv3OVzYGE/a7YwiopVDxkUpV852qbXFA/watch?embed
Video: Rearranging the Cosine Rule to find angles (SSS) (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAGv3EOdsGw/cLFitrNl8G-pLT4vybMXVA/watch?embed
activities: Use the Cosine Rule to calculate one missing side given two sides and the included angle (SAS).
Use the Cosine Rule (rearranged) to calculate one missing angle given all three sides (SSS).
Explain how the formula becomes Pythagoras’ Theorem when the included angle is 90°.
Challenge prompt: What’s always true about this formula?

human_story.focus: Connect the Cosine Rule to triangulation in navigation, astronomy, and surveying, where direct measurement is impossible and accuracy matters for safety and decision-making.
justice_prompts: When have humans had to estimate distances without being able to measure directly?
Where might this matter in real life or history? What happens if we get it wrong?

expressive_self.focus: Build intuition by explaining the Cosine Rule in your own words and representing it visually, noticing whether finding a side or an angle feels more intuitive.
activities: Write your own explanation of how the Cosine Rule extends Pythagoras’ Theorem.
Draw a labelled triangle showing when you’d use the Cosine Rule and annotate it.
Reflect: Which feels more intuitive — finding a side or an angle? Why?

designed_world.focus: Use interactive sketches to solve SAS and SSS triangle problems and connect the skill to design and engineering contexts where triangle calculations guide real decisions.
design_challenges: Use the sketch to reveal/hide angles and sides to create your own SAS problems and solve them.
Use the sketch to create SSS problems and calculate missing angles by rearranging the Cosine Rule.
Challenge a classmate: “Find angle A or side b using only what you see.”
Consider: what types of design problems (architecture, surveying, robotics) rely on this kind of calculation?

regulation_supports: After working on triangle problems, trace a triangle shape in the air with your hand while breathing in slowly.
Stretch your arms outward like triangle sides, then lower them as you exhale.
Notice tension in your neck or shoulders and gently roll them out.
wellbeing_prompts: Where do you feel effort in your body when solving something tricky? What helps you reset?

pathways.name: Final Task: Real World Triangles (SDG 11)
pathways.focus: Use both forms of the Cosine Rule (SAS and SSS) to solve a park design triangle layout, then explain reasoning and propose an accessibility or sustainability improvement.
products: A labelled triangle layout created in the web sketch tool (screenshots).
Working showing Cosine Rule to find a missing side (SAS).
Working showing Cosine Rule to find a missing angle (SSS).
A short reflection including one way the design could support accessibility or sustainability.
Self and peer assessment using the rubric.

required: Pythagoras’ Theorem and right-angled triangle reasoning
Basic trig ratios in right-angled triangles (sin, cos, tan)
Using a calculator for cosine values (degree mode where appropriate)
Rearranging equations and working accurately with squares and square roots

builds_toward: Area of a triangle using 1/2ab sin(C)
